subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Sunspots and the Solar Cycle
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Sunspots and the Solar Cycle.

hook.text: Dark blotches appear on the Sun's surface, grow, then fade. They follow an 11-year rhythm.

buildup.text: Sunspots are cooler regions caused by intense magnetic activity. They can be larger than Earth.

discovery.text: Galileo observed them through a telescope in 1612, proving the Sun is not a perfect, unchanging sphere.

twist.text: From 1645 to 1715, sunspots nearly vanished. Europe froze. The link to climate is still debated.

climax.text: At solar maximum, sunspots peak and flares surge. At solar minimum, the Sun goes quiet for years.

punchline.text: Even stars have moods on an 11-year clock.

quiz.question: What did the disappearance of sunspots from 1645 to 1715 coincide with?
options: A period of unusually cold temperatures in Europe
A dramatic increase in solar flare activity
The discovery of new planets in the solar system
